text.title: ba-requirements-mgmt Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for ba-requirements-mgmt. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Baseline requirements using a deterministic tag (e.g., `req-baseline-2026-Q2`) so that "what changed" is always answerable with one `git diff` command, not a meeting.
rule.rationale: Baseline tags are the auditable record of scope; without them the change history is narrative and unverifiable.

rule.statement: Assign mandatory attributes to every requirement: status (draft, approved, implemented), priority, owner (person, not team), source, last_review date.
rule.rationale: Mandatory attributes are the minimal set to detect staleness, ownership gaps, and when a requirement drifts from its source intent.

rule.statement: Periodic review cadence: flag requirements with stale `last_review` (>90 days), missing owner, or no traceability link; open a PR with `needs_review: true`.
rule.rationale: Without periodic review requirements decay invisibly; the owner field forces a named person to be accountable for freshness.

rule.statement: Archive on tag, not delete: a quarterly tag `req-archive-2026-Q2` snapshots retired REQs; remove from active `requirements/` so daily search stays fast.
rule.rationale: Deletion loses audit trail; archival preserves history while keeping the active set searchable.

rule.statement: Make CIA mandatory for any CR touching ≥2 requirements; under that, allow a one-line note. The threshold prevents form fatigue and focuses rigor on high-impact changes.
rule.rationale: Small changes introduce bureaucracy costs faster than they reduce risk; large changes need explicit assessment.

rule.statement: For each CR, assess impact on five areas: requirements touched (count), design components, code modules, test cases, total effort. Use T-shirt sizes (S/M/L/XL) by area, not hours — convert to hours only at planning time.
rule.rationale: T-shirt sizes reduce false precision; hours are guesses dressed as commitments.

rule.statement: CIA generation is deterministic walk + LLM phrasing: do NOT invent impact links; use only what is in the traceability JSON. If traceability is empty for a given REQ, return `effort: null, reason: "no trace data"` and refuse to estimate.
rule.rationale: LLMs hallucinate impacted artifacts when trace data is incomplete; leaving the number empty flags the knowledge gap.

rule.statement: Identify risks (technical, schedule, quality) and stakeholder impact per CR. For each risk, assign probability (H/M/L) and impact (H/M/L); propose mitigation.
rule.rationale: Risk assessment forces explicit hazard identification before approval; unseen risks degrade to post-incident surprises.

rule.statement: Decompose requirements into a hierarchy with a hard cap on depth (e.g., 3 levels: BR → SR → FR). Anything deeper is task decomposition and belongs in the implementation plan.
rule.rationale: Deep hierarchies create maintenance burden; 3 levels cover all coherent scope abstractions.

rule.statement: Use four viewpoints (business, user, technical, operational) to partition requirements. Assign single primary viewpoint per requirement plus optional secondary views as labels; avoid duplication across views.
rule.rationale: Multiple viewpoints allow different stakeholder perspectives; a clear primary viewpoint prevents ambiguity.

rule.statement: Render the requirement hierarchy and dependency graph (Mermaid / Graphviz) on every commit. Detect and flag orphans (no parent), cycles (dependency loops), and single-child chains (collapse candidates).
rule.rationale: Automated graph detection scales beyond manual review; visual diffs make structural changes explicit.

rule.statement: Architecture agents must never create new hierarchy nodes to "fix" orphans. Only flag and report; let humans decide whether to reparent, delete, or accept the orphan.
rule.rationale: Automatic repair invents scope; agents can identify but not decide.
